SPO/E document and tag mappings
SPO/E documents and tags might need to be exchanged frequently between EPCs and Owner Operators during the plant lifecycle. There can be different types of documents like engineering, vendor, and procurement, and different types
of tags like electrical, instrument, and telecom. The mappings in this section illustrate the creation of import and export mappings for engineering documents and instrument tags. You can extend these mappings and use them for creating
mappings for different types of documents and tags.
Document mappings
A physical column maps to a specific column in the input CSV file. There may be cases where a value that is required for a mapping is either not available in the physical file or the physical data supplied needs to be reformatted. In such
cases, a computed column can be set up. For example, you may need to create a specific type of SPO/E engineering document depending upon the document type class definition provided in the CSV file.
We recommend that all mappings are prefixed.
If an object, property, or relationship in the target system already exists in the staging area, then you must rename the object, property, or relationship when saving the respective mapping. For example, when mapping the Area
column header to the SPFFunctionalArea classification, you must modify the class definition name by adding a prefix such as SDV. You would then type SDVSPFFunctionalArea in the Maps to Object box.
The Relationship Mapping option is not enabled until at least two objects have been mapped.
You can use the SmartPlant Foundation SmartConverter to hotspot drawings. For more information, see Configuring the SmartConverter for Graphical Navigation.
When creating a job for exporting the SPO/E documents to the target system, you must suppress the ENS. Otherwise, the documents you are exporting will be assigned ENS generated names in the target system and the
relationship between the tags you are exporting and these documents may not be established. To suppress the ENS, in Data Validator Job Management, select the Suppress ENS checkbox in the Create New Job dialog box.
Use the following illustration and tables to create your own document mapping.

Validation rules
The AutoGeneratedRuleSet rule set is displayed in the RulesSets pane if you select the Auto Generate Validation Rules option during the creation of the import definition.
Export mapping
The export mapping is displayed in the Export Mapping pane if you select the Auto Generate Export Mapping option during the creation of the import definition.
You must specify the object weight for each Stage to Target Object Map available under the export mapping. This is required to load the data available in the CSV file to the target system. Objects with lower weights are processed first.
For example, the object weights must be set to export the documents before exporting the tags.

Export file mappings
When you export files to a target system, the import mapping maps a file object type, (for example SPFDesignFile) and creates a property with a Check Staging File Exists action, which is attached with the full file path of the file stored in
SPFLocalFileName.
During the export process, all of the local staging files found are uploaded to the target system file services. The export process then creates all of the design files and saves the files into the vaults.
This mapping uses the Check Target System File Exists action, which ensures that the file is uploaded correctly using the path that is accessible by the target system server.
The target system loading will stop an export that contains an object with more than one property with a Check Staging File Exists or Check Target System File Exists action. This is because an object that represents a file can only
have one file attached to it in SmartPlant Foundation. Additionally, the export process checks for any updates to an existing file object and warns the user that those updates are not supported. These update warnings are ignored so that
the load can continue.
CSV mapping example
The following CSV example mappings are an extension of the Document mappings. However, this section only shows the extensions to work with files and does not list the document mappings.

Files with custom target systems
If you have a target system that does not use SmartPlant Foundation, Data Validator provides the ability to validate that the files exist in the target system.
Data Validator attempts to send the physical files from the staging side to the target system when:
The mapping contains properties with the CheckTargetSystemFileExists action.
The mapping contains a FileComposition relationship between the file object and the object it is attached to.
The target system returns True from the SupportsPhysicalFileHandling API.
The target system has a FileService configured to receive the files.
Creating new versions and copying files from the previous version
When the same file is available in the input CSV file for subsequent imports, the document version is created depending on the default settings in the Job Definition dialog box on the Export Configuration page.
Example 1
Scenario: File1 already exists in the database. The Allow Version Creation option is set to True. Input CSV file with information about File1 is loaded.
Result: A new version V2 is created and the File1 from the input CSV file is attached to version V2.
Example 2
Scenario: File1 already exists in the database. The Allow Version Creation and Copy Files from the Previous Version options are set to True. Input CSV file with information about File2 is loaded.
Result: A new version V2 is created. The File1 is copied from the previous version (V1) and the File2 are both attached to version V2.
Example 3
Scenario: File1 already exists in the database. The Allow Version Creation option is set to True and Copy Files from the Previous Version option is set to False. Input CSV file with information about File2 is loaded.
Result: A new version V2 is created and the File2 is attached to version V2.
Mappings for supported files
When you export files to a target system, the import mappings search for the design file, markup file, navigation file, graphics file, and alternate rendition files. The design file is then attached to the revision object in the staging system and
the other files are attached to the design file, except for the graphics map file, which is attached to the navigation file.
During the export process, local staging files are uploaded to the target system file services, and various design files are created and saved in a vault.
This mapping uses the Check Target System File Exists action, which ensures that the file is uploaded correctly using the path accessible by the target system server.

To identify supporting file objects, use query definition as .Name for SPFDesignFile, SPFMarkupFile, SPFAlternateRendition, SPFNavigationFile, and SPFGraphicsMap.
The design file must exist before the markup and navigation files in the Target system. The export weightings must be set to export the design file before the markup file and navigation file, and the navigation file before the graphics
map. The navigation file must be exported before the graphics map file.

Data Validator mapping export
You can export the mappings, validation rulesets, and job definitions created in one Data Validator system to another system using the Data Validator Mapping Export Utility. Mappings are exported as XML load files using Desktop Client,
but the objects and relationships must be converted before the mappings can be loaded into another system.
The Data Validator Mapping Export Utility converts the XML load files to NLF format and removes UIDs from the files. This avoids clash problems in databases with existing mappings. You need to delete the column headers of an existing
import map before loading the NLF file using the Loader in Desktop Client.
The Data Validator Mapping Export Utility and accompanying documentation is delivered on Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more information, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
Delete job definitions using the Desktop Client
You can delete a job definition from the Data Validator system using the Desktop Client. However, if any relationships exist between the job definition and jobs being run through a Data Validator Job Management workflow, the option to
delete is not available. You can also delete a job definition using Data Validator Administration. For more information, see Delete a job definition.
1. In Data Validator Administration, clear the Is Enabled option in the job definition so it cannot be used to create any new jobs in Data Validator Job Management. For more information, see Edit a job definition.
2. In Data Validator Job Management, delete any jobs that used the job definition to be deleted. For more information, see Sort your jobs.
3. Open the Desktop Client on a computer which has Data Validator fully installed.
4. Click Find > Data Validator > Job Definition.
5. Using the Find Job Definition dialog box, enter the name of the job definition or a wildcard character, and click OK.
6. Right-click the job definition to be deleted, and click Delete.
Supported CSV file formats
Data Validator supports the data import from two types of CSV file formats, which allows the same validation rules to be used regardless of the imported data file and the exported data to be in the same invariant format as the target
system.
CSV file format.
CSV raw attribute file format (inverted file format).
 For more information on using computed columns and functions, see Use the Computed column.
Each CSV file format is explained as follows:
CSV file format
The CSV file format is a standard delimited text file that uses a comma, or other separators, to separate values for columns in a spreadsheet. For example:
This information is shown in the staging area as shown in the following illustration:
CSV Raw Attribute file format (inverted file format)
CSV Raw Attribute file format is used to load design properties. This is when a single object has many properties that makes it impractical to represent each property in its own column. Each row in the CSV Raw Attribute file format refers
to a different property definition, whereas the property definition is named in the column mapping for a standard CSV file. For example:
This information is shown in the staging area as shown in the following illustration:
CSV files not in invariant format
What is invariant format?
Invariant format is a property that does not change when a specified transformation is applied on a CSV file. For example, Data Validator uses the invariant format for thousand separator “,” and for decimal separator “.”. So a value of 1.001
would always be interpreted as 1 with three decimal places even if your intention may have been 1001, as the decimal point was really a thousand separator. Data Validator will not return any validation errors on properties using the
invariant format, but you run the risk of not getting the intended data into the system.
For more information on CSV files not in invariant format, see CSV files not in invariant format.
Invariant format CSV files
If the data in a CSV file is not supplied in invariant format, it is possible that Data Validator will interpret the data as valid data and not return any validation errors on the export of the data. Therefore, you run a risk of not getting the intended
data into the target system. For example, submitting a value of 1.001 would be interpreted as 1 with three decimal places, when the intention may have been 1001, as the decimal point was really a thousand separator. Similar conditions
can occur with dates.
If you import a CSV file that is not in invariant format, you must use computed columns to convert the data to invariant format. This applies to all dates, time offsets, and numerics in localized format.
 Data Validator uses the invariant format as defined by SmartPlant Foundation as follows:
Date Time Format: yyyy/MM/dd - HH:mm:ss:fff
Timezone: GMT with no day light savings adjustment
Year Month Day Format: yyyy/MM/dd
Numeric format:
Thousand separator ","
Decimal separator "."
For more information on using computed columns and functions, see Use the Computed column and Functions.
Clash detection
Data Validator has advanced clash detection capabilities, which ensure that clashes between the imported schema and the existing local schema are detected so that corrective action can be applied. Data Validator works only with the
data in the Data Validator domain, because it has control over those database tables and can transfer the data for each job into a new set of tables.
When you build an import mapping from the target system that maps any reserved class definitions, property definitions, relationship definitions, (such as an interface definition or a property definition), or relationship property definitions,
the system automatically detects clashes and it displays a warning message to take corrective action, such as renaming the class definition.
For example, the following image is an example of a definition for ClassDef, which is a reserved type that is unusable for Data Validator.
Here, ClassDef is linked to a component schema, which in turn is linked to a domain, which in turn links to a domain group. The domain group has a property that names the table prefix. For this example, the domain group is named
SCHEMA.
This example does not point to the Data Validator tables, but points to the SCHEMA tables, which means that Data Validator cannot use this class definition, as doing so would create a clash between what is being mapped and what is
already in the Data Validator database.
If you do intend to introduce a new class definition into your target system, use the following example workflow:
1. Create an import mapping by querying the target system.
2. Query for the object type, for example, ClassDef.
3. Click Add 
 to create the mapping, and the application will check for clashes.
4. If a class definition, property definition, or relationship property definition clashes, the application creates a list of alternatives by searching the stage to target map to determine whether the class definition, property definition, or
relationship property definition has already been targeted. If it has, the local schema is presented as the alternative. For example, if a map exists from the local class definition ClassDef to a target class definition ClassDef, then the
local class definition ClassDef will be suggested as an alternative.
5. If a clash is found, choose an alternative class definition from the list provided by the application. If no list of alternatives is provided, use free text to rename the object type, renaming ClassDef to MyClassDef.
6. Click Save.
7. When you make use of the Auto Generate Export Mapping option, the local object is named MyClassDef, but when MyClassDef is exported, it is exported as ClassDef.
Similar to clashes between class or property definitions, clashes can occur between relationship definitions, because the Data Validator object types do not have the same interfaces as the objects in the target system. Relationship
definitions link instances of interfaces, so when importing a relationship definition local to Data Validator, the relationship definition is mapped to the primary interfaces of the two objects being linked. A clash occurs when the relationship
definition is found locally, but links two different interfaces. Data Validator looks at the end objects and matches to the objects you want to use that already exist in the Data Validator database.
This condition can also occur for properties, which should only be exposed by one interface. If you target a property that exists in the Data Validator database, but belongs to an interface not found on the associated object, a clash occurs.
You will be prompted to alter the property name. Similar clash detection and resolution can occur in the case of relationship properties.
 This clash detection functionality is also used when mapping using the free text mode.
 You can create new relationship definitions in addition to the existing relationship definition in the system between different interface definitions.
Export objects configured with ENS in the target system
The Engineering Numbering System (ENS) provides automated object identification, which allows project-specific identification formats to be configured as templates. These templates guide the user when creating new objects.
The ENS definition is composed of various components called ENS items that can extract data from constants and from other properties on the object or related objects. These components are configured in sequence with additional
delimiter characters to define the required format.
If a class definition is configured to use ENS in the target system database, then the object name is set by the ENS definition instead of using the actual name of the object provided in the CSV file during export.
If the ENS definition is related to a property definition, such as Name, and this property definition is referred to in a UID or unique key, then Data Validator fails to identify the object and leads to export failures. This is because the
relationships are processed as the UIDs or unique keys and these get changed according to the ENS definition.
 It is not recommended to run jobs in parallel when using ENS because it might lead to data corruption and unexpected results.
Configuration for a tag object
To map a tag object available in the CSV file with the tag object in the target system database, do the following:
Ensure the DevTag class definition optionally realizes the ISPFENSRefDetails interface. This interface exposes the ENSRefName property to store the tag name from the CSV file.
<Rel>
 <IObject UID="DEVTag.Realizes.ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 <ISchemaObj />
 <IRel UID1="DEVTag" DefUID="Realizes" UID2="ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 </Rel>
Create an import mapping with a computed column, as shown in the example below.
Map the tag name from the CSV file to the ENSRefName property of the tag object.
Get the actual tag name from the target system, and map it to the Name property.
 The ENSRefName property definition is used to store the original name of the tag object from the CSV file and helps Data Validator identify the tag object within the target system during the update, delete, and terminate object
actions.
For example, if ENS is configured on the Name property of a tag, such as [Current Config][-][TG][-][Sequence], and TAG1 is exported to the target system where the configuration is PlantA and 'TG' is a constant, then the name of the tag in
the target system is set to PlantA-TG-00001 and the ENSRefName property to TAG1.
Example CSV file
Object mapping example
Column header
Column type
Object mappings
Object name
TAG NAME
Physical
DevTag
DevTag
CLASS
Physical
SPFEquipmentClass
SDVEquipmentClass
AREA
Physical
SPFFunctionalArea
SDVFunctionalArea
SYSTEM
Physical
SPFSystem
SDVSystem
CONTRACT
Physical
SPFContract
SDVContract
Property mapping example
Column header
Column type
Property mappings
Parent objects
COMP_Actual_TS_Tag_Name
Computed
Name (Create Update action)
TAG NAME
COMP_Tag_Ref_Name
Computed
ENSRefName (Create Update action)
TAG NAME
Computed column for COMP_Actual_TS_Tag_Name
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("true", [TAGNAME], "#DEVTag,.ENSRefName=[TAGNAME]", ".Name")
Computed column for COMP_Tag_Ref_Name
func.Concat({[TAG NAME]})
Configuration for a document object
If you have configured ENS for a document master, revision, and/or version, the SPFDesignDocMaster and the SPFDesignDocRevision class definitions must optionally realize the ISPFENSRefDetails interface definition, which
exposes the ENSRefName property. The document number value must be set on the master object’s ENSRefName property. The document number, major revision, and minor revision must be set on the ENSRefName property of the
revision object.
To map a document object available in the CSV file with the document object in the target system database, do the following:
Ensure the ISPFENSRefDetails interface is optionally realized by the SPFDesignDocMaster and SPFDesignDocRevision class definitions. This interface exposes the ENSRefName property to store the document name and
revision code from the CSV file.
<Rel>
 <IObject UID="SPFDesignDocMaster.Realizes.ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 <ISchemaObj />
 <IRel UID1="SPFDesignDocMaster" DefUID="Realizes" UID2="ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 </Rel>
 <Rel>
 <IObject UID="SPFDesignDocRevision.Realizes.ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 <ISchemaObj />
 <IRel UID1="SPFDesignDocRevision" DefUID="Realizes" UID2="ISPFENSRefDetails"/>
</Rel>
Create an import mapping with a computed column, as shown in the example below.
Map the document name from the CSV file to the ENSRefName property of the document master object.
Map the document name and the revision code to the ENSRefName property of the document revision object.
Get the actual document name from the target system, and map it to the Name property of the document master and revision object.
 The files attached to the document can be identified using the query definition, +SPFFileComposition.ENSRefName,.Name. This query definition needs to be modified, for your staging schema.

Case-insensitive support for Oracle databases
The case-insensitivity of the database interactions in Data Validator are dependent on the value set for the CaseInsensitiveOracle property in the Settings node for a site in Server Manager. It is recommended to have the same value set
for the CaseInsensitiveOracle property in the staging and target systems.
 In order to use case-insensitive support, you must configure the export mapping using query definitions to find objects in the target system.
The value set for the CaseInsensitiveOracle property does not apply to the schema items, UoM values, and enum values.
Keyword searches with the criteria specified using the unique key and UID are case-sensitive.
The Run Inconsistency Property Values option in the Create Job Definition dialog box checks the value of the CaseInsensitiveOracle property of the staging system for the case-sensitivity of the rule execution.
Validation rules
The following table lists all the validation rules supplied with Data Validator and the system types that are considered for the case-sensitiveness of the rule execution.

Target system

Target system

Target system

Target system

Check Properties Against Classification
Not applicable (case-sensitive because it searches for schema items)

Not applicable (case-sensitive because the search criteria uses the unique key)

Implicit delete rules
The implicit delete rules use the value set in the CaseInsensitiveOracle property of the target system for the case-sensitivity of the rule execution.
 For SQL Server databases, the case-sensitivity of Data Validator is based on the collation settings. You must set the case-sensitive or case-insensitive settings while installing the database. This setting will determine
whether or not Data Validator will consider case during rule execution.
Job reports
You can use Data Validator to store the information related to a job in the database. The stored information can be used as a metadata for the generation of reports. For this purpose, a new schema is provided to record the job information,
such as Import Details, Validation, Implicit Delete, and Export Details. If you delete a job from the target system, only that job is deleted, its details are still stored in the database. This happens only if you select the Record Job Details
option when creating a job definition, and associate it with that specific job. You can find the job summary details in the staging system.
The following image shows the new class definitions and the corresponding property definitions that are modeled to hold job details.
The following class definitions are delivered with the software.
SDVJobSummary - Stores the basic details of a job, such as Import Stage Details and Validation Stage Details.
SDVJobSummarySeverityItem - Displays the severity items of a job, such as warnings, errors, and the count.
SDVJobSummaryTarget - Stores export stage details and implicit delete stage details of each target system.
SDVJobSummaryImportFile - Stores import file that is used to execute a Job.
Each class definition contains property definitions that allows you to define the properties of a job, such as name, description, and other properties. The SDVJobSummarySeverityItem, SDVJobSummaryTarget, and
SDVJobSummaryImportFile class definitions are associated with the main class definition SDVJobSummary.
The job details are stored in the staging system even after deletion of the job from Data Validator.
To view job details, you must be working in a role associated with the VTLAdmin access group.
For example, create a job definition, named Test by applying Record Job Details option. For more information, refer to Create a job definition. Use this job definition to create a new job named Job1.
The following job details are stored in the staging system. If you delete the job Job1, its job details will still be available in the staging system.
 Select the Record Job Details option to store the job information such as Import details, Validation, Export details and other job information.
EPC database registration after project completion
When a project completes, an EPC may hand over the project database to the Owner Operator. The number of EPC databases that are handed over, as well as the tools that the databases are from determine whether the tool signature for
the database can be re-registered to the Owner Operator’s SmartPlant Foundation site.
If only one EPC is handing over the database, then the tool signature can be re-registered to the Owner Operator's SmartPlant Foundation site.
If more than one EPC is handing over databases for tools other than Smart 3D, then only one of the databases can be registered to the Owner Operator’s SmartPlant Foundation site.
If more than one EPC is handing over databases with a Smart 3D tool signature, all of the EPC databases can be registered to the Owner Operator’s SmartPlant Foundation site.
 Smart 3D is the only tool that supports multiple registrations to the same SmartPlant Foundation site.
Exporting Data Validator configurations
EPCs and Owner Operators frequently have to exchange data incrementally based on the progress they make when collaborating on projects. In such cases, it is a cumbersome task to load all the configurations like import mappings,
export mappings, rulesets, job definitions, and so on as separate XML files into the target system multiple times when changes are made.
You can use the Export Data Validator Configurations and the Compare XML Values options in the staging system Desktop Client and generate the delta changes as XML files in a couple of clicks. After verifying that the changes are
correctly captured, the XML files can be loaded into the target system to update the data with all the changes.
 The schema files are not compared as the comparison may generate the delete instructions for the staging schema which is referenced in another configuration.
Generate the base configuration
1. In the Desktop Client, click Find > Data Validator > Job Definition.
2. In the Find Job Definition dialog box, enter the name of the job definition or a wildcard character, and click OK.
3. Right-click the selected job definition, and click Export Data Validator Configurations.
4. In the Export Data dialog box, ensure the Split Files by Container ID checkbox is not selected, and click Export.
 Before clicking Export, we recommend you to clear the Split Files by Container ID checkbox to avoid creation of multiple files based on container id, so that there are less number of files to compare.
5. In the Browse for Folder dialog box, navigate to the location where you want to save the folder containing the exported data.
The software creates the folder, MultipleContainerIDs, containing three files - SCHEMA_MultipleContainerIDs.xml, ADMIN_MultipleContainerIDs.xml, and DATA_MultipleContainerIDs.xml.
 Consider this folder and these XML files as the base configuration.
Generate the modified configuration
1. Make the required changes to the Data Validator configuration and repeat steps 1 - 5 above.
 Consider the folder and the XML files created now as the modified configuration.
Compare the base and modified configurations
In the Desktop Client, use the Compare XML Files option to compare the ADMIN and DATA XML files in the base and modified configurations, and generate instructions to migrate the data in the database based on the differences
between the files.
1. Select Administration > Compare XML Files in the Desktop Client to open the Compare XML Files dialog box.
2. In the File 1 box, browse to select the ADMIN_MultipleContainerIDs.xml file from the base configuration folder.
3. In the File 2 box, browse to select the ADMIN_MultipleContainerIDs.xml file from the modified configuration folder.
4. Click OK. The Xml Comparison form shows the differences between the two admin files highlighted in different colors (see key at the bottom of the dialog box).
5. Use the Previous and Next buttons to step from one difference to the next.
 Review all the additions, deletions, and changes carefully.
6. Click Generate Instructions to migrate the data in the database based on the differences between the files and the associated instructions for loading.
7. Click Exit.
8. Repeat steps 1 to 7 to generate loading instructions for the DATA_MultipleContainerIDs.xml files in the base and modified configurations.
Load the incremental changes into the target system
Use the Desktop Client Loader in the target system to load the generated instructions from the comparison of the ADMIN_MultipleContainerIDs.xml and DATA_MultipleContainerIDs.xml files. For more information, see Loader.
 The updated schema files can be directly loaded without comparing the files.
SmartPlant Access Control Analysis
Intergraph SmartPlant Access Control Analysis is a richly featured reporting and administration application that can quickly and easily visualize and edit access control data that includes methods, client APIs, relationship definitions, and
access groups. The access control data can then be used to tune the security and access configuration of the SmartPlant Foundation system. For example:
You can find each object through the details exposed through related items using a dynamic query mechanism.
You can find a role that is related to one or more access groups and the access groups are related to one or more methods or relationship definitions.
You can easily identify all the capabilities that a given role or access group can provide to a user.
You can edit and review delivered access groups and their duplicates.
For more information on how the security model can be configured for a SmartPlant Foundation system, see Security Model Configuration Overview.
Access control query wizard
You can quickly and easily find access information in SmartPlant Access Control Analysis using the Access Control Query Wizard, with which you define specific details to build a query that displays such information as:
Methods
Relationship Definitions
Graph Definitions
Edge Definitions
View Definitions
Access Groups
You can also use SmartPlant Access Control Analysis to find more detailed information, such as:
Identify the methods exposed on a right-hand mouse function
Find any method that does not have access control
Show any methods that have a toolbar item
Report which methods are exposed to a user or a role
View how many methods a specific role has access to
Display all relationship definitions for an interface definition
Visualize all the content of an access group, such as methods and relationship definitions
Compare one access group with another and make changes based on that comparison
Query types
You build a query based on the main query type that you initially select to find the access control data you want. The main query types are as follows:
By User Profile
This query type uses specific access types, such as users, roles, access groups, configuration items, and the starting object type.
This diagram represents a query By User Profile that shows how methods can be found through the user profile. This query is based on using Methods as the starting object and selecting an access group as the main criteria type. You
can then refine the results as you define the query by selecting different filters, such as specific configurations, interface definitions and client APIs. By doing so, you access a targeted set of manageable data.
By Schema
This query uses specific schema objects, such as class definitions, interface definitions, or the starting object type.
This diagram represents a query By Schema to locate methods from a class definition through their interface definitions. This query is based on using Methods as the starting object and selecting a class definition as the main criteria type.
You can refine the results as you define the query by selecting different filters, such as specific configurations, interface definitions and client APIs. By doing so, you access a targeted set of manageable data.
All Methods
This query type is only available if you select Methods as the starting object type, and uses client APIs and additional filters to locate the methods.
This diagram represents a query using All Methods. The methods are found by selecting specific client APIs to narrow your results and then using the additional filtering, such as if the client API is found in the Desktop Client. By doing so,
you access a targeted set of manageable data.
The access data query results are returned in the main window displayed in columns in a tabular grid view. Further options are available where you can:
Refine the query results to reduce the data set to a more manageable, targeted set of data.
Open all of the results or select a single item as a paged query in the Desktop Client.
Display the access control details of a single item as a diagram, where you can visualize all of the relationships for the item as a diagrammatic representation.
For more information, see Explore the access control query results and View the access control diagram.
Getting started in SmartPlant Access Control Analysis
You can query for access control data with SmartPlant Access Control Analysis by building a query using two query types:
Access Control Query
Access Group Query
Each query dynamically locates data in the system database using configurable settings in a wizard and displays the results in an easily navigated window.
To get started in SmartPlant Access Control Analysis, log on to the SmartPlant Dashboard, and click the Access Control Analysis tile on the main window.
You then select the starting object tile for the type of access data that you want to build a query:
Access Groups
Edge Definitions
Graph Definitions
Methods
Relationship Definitions
View Definitions
Build an access control query
You can now build an access control query for the starting object type using the Access Control Query Wizard. The wizard displays the following pages:
Query types
The Query Type page is where you can select a query type to build the access control query as follows:
By User Profile - Selects a user, a role, a configuration, an access group, or the starting object as the query type.
By Schema - Selects a specific class definition, an interface definition, or the starting object as the query type.
All Methods - Selects methods as your starting object type. You can use client APIs to narrow the results.
Main criteria
The Main Criteria page is where you choose the criteria to build the query. The available options are sensitive to the starting object and query type selected.
Type a name in the Main Criteria Name box or search for the object using wildcards. For example, in a By User Profile query type, search and select the name of a user, such as Piping Engineer.
Filter
The filter pages displayed are sensitive to the context of any preceding pages. Each page shows a list of filters that you can apply to narrow the results, such as Configuration Items, Roles, Access Groups, Interfaces, and Client APIs.
 Click Select All to select all items in a filter list or click Deselect All to remove them from the list so you can then select items individually.
Additional filters
The Additional Filters page displays a set of filters to further refine the query criteria depending on the query type selected. The filter options are sensitive to the context of any preceding pages. For more information, see Additional Query
Filters.
Summary
The Summary page is where you can confirm the details of the search query are correct or go back to the previous pages and edit the query. Click Finish to run the query.
How can I refine the access control query?
After you have run a query, you can refine the access group query by clicking 
 Refine Query. This re-opens the query so you can edit the details using the Access Groups Query Wizard.
For examples on how the pages are displayed using different query types in the wizard, see Access Control Query Examples.
If an object is not showing in a query result list, such as method, access to these can be controlled using access groups. Contact your administrator for more information on your access permissions.
Explore the access control query results
When the access control query has been run, the starting object results are displayed alphabetically in columns in a results grid view in the main window.
The following example shows the results grid for a query for methods using the query type By User profile with the user Datawarehouse Editor as the main criteria.
The displayed columns vary depending on the selected starting object type, query type, and main criteria defined for the access query.
You can filter and sort the results in each column, see Filtering, Sorting and Saving Results.
Each column set is fixed for each object type.
The following list shows some of the column headers that a query could potentially display:
[Object] Name
Description
API Name
Access Group
Role
Owning Groups
User
Condition
Interface Definition
Class Definition
Relationship Definition
GUI Items
Group the access control query results
You can group the access control query results together using a selected column set. This displays all the objects found in the column set grouped alphabetically in a list.
To group the displayed results by column set, drag a selected column header on to the Drag a column header and drop it here to group by that column area.
For example, the following image shows the results for the access control for relationship definitions By Schema query type, grouped by the Relationship Definitions Name column set.
The total Count of each object found in the column is shown in each row. You can click on a row to expand the individual objects listed and view the object's information found in other columns, such as shown in the DEVTagTag row.
Open the results in the Desktop Client
You can open the items in the Desktop Client from the results grid view, as well as from an access control diagram. You can either send a single item or a group of items as a paged query to the Desktop Client.
To open and view your selected results in the Desktop Client, click one of the following options on the main window toolbar:
 Open page in SmartPlant Foundation Client - Opens all of the items displayed in a single paged query in the Desktop Client.
 Open selected items in SmartPlant Foundation Client - Opens a selected item or a selected set of items in the Desktop Client.
 If your selection contains any duplicate objects in a query, only a single instance is shown in Desktop Client.
View the access control diagram
The Access Control Diagram is a graphical representation of the data created from a single object. The diagram displays the object's details in a dynamic display that shows any related items in separate nodes, such as the roles, access
groups, and client APIs.
To display an object in an Access Control Diagram, select a single object in a row, and click 
 Show Access Control Diagram. You can rearrange the nodes in the diagram, as well as use the following controls to customize the
diagram to your requirements.
Icon
Name
Description
Zoom
Increases the size of the diagram by percentage scale.
Pan
Drags a complete diagram to a new position in the window.
Move
Selects an item node and move its position.
Fit All
Resizes the diagram to fit the window.
More..
Expand
Expands the contents of an item node to show a full list of objects.
Open
Opens a selected item node in the Desktop Client.
Print
Send the diagram to a printer or save as a file.
For examples of access control diagrams, see Access Control Diagram Examples.
Build an access group query
Access groups are the primary objects used to configure security in a SmartPlant Foundation system. You can edit access groups to control the different levels of functional access for users, such as method access to menu commands. An
access group query can:
Compare one access group with another and edit the content based on the comparison.
Create a new access group and configure the content using an existing access group as the source.
Copy an existing access group and manipulate the content of the duplicate.
You can build an access group query by selecting Manage Access Groups as the starting object type to open the Access Groups Query Wizard. The main pages that display are:
Access Groups page
On the Access Groups page select a Target Access Group and a Source Access Group.
Target Access Group
Click Select an existing Access Group.
Type the name or search using wildcards and select an existing access group from the list.
Click Create a new Access Group.
Type the details for the new access group in the Name and Description boxes and select the required Configuration Statuses from the list.
Source Access Group
Select the name of the Source Access Group.
Type the name, or search using wildcards, and select an existing access group from the list.
Summary page
Review the query details in the Summary page. You can go back and edit the query details, or click Finish to run the query.
For examples on how the pages are displayed in the wizard, see Access Control Query Examples.
For further information on access groups, see Access group configuration.
Explore the access group query results
After you have run a Manage Access Groups query, the query results are displayed in two access group list view panes.
Target Access Group - Displays the target object types in the left pane.
Source Access Group - Displays the source object types in the right pane.
 You can refine the access group query by clicking 
 Refine Query. This opens the query so you can edit the details and re-run the query.
The following example shows the list panes for a Manage Access Groups query that uses ConfigAdmin as the source access group and AuthoringAdministrator as the target access group.
You can filter and sort the results in each column, see Filtering, Sorting and Saving Results.
Each column set is fixed for each object type.
View the access group object types
The selected target and source access groups display their results in columns under several object type tabs:
Methods tab
The details of each method is displayed in both the Target Access Group and the Source Access Group panes in the following columns:
Methods Name - The name of the method whose behavior is driven by arguments defined on a client API, such as a shortcut menu item.
Restrict to workflow recipients - Indicates if the method access is restricted when the object is in a workflow, such as when the method is only available to users that are action recipients of the current workflow.
Filter by owner or owning group - Indicates if the method is filtered by either the owner or the owning group, restricting access to GUI menu items.
Condition - The condition configured on the method, which must be satisfied when access group users try to run the method.
Relationship Definitions tab
The details of each relationship definition is displayed in both the Target Access Group and Source Access Group panes in the following columns:
Relationship Definition Name - The name of the relationship definition associated between interface definitions.
Expand 1-2 - Indicates that the relationship can be navigated by expanding forwards from end 1 to end 2.
Expand 2-1 - indicate that the relationship can be navigated by expanding backwards from end 2 to end 1.
Drag-Drop 1-2 - Indicates that the relationship definition allows the dropping of an object at end 1 onto end 2.
Drag-Drop 2-1 - Indicates that the relationship definition allows the dropping of an object at end 2 onto end 1.
Terminate - Indicates if the relationship definition is configured to allow access to terminate its status.
Drag-Drop Condition - The condition that controls the relationship dropping of one object onto another.
Expand 1-2 Condition - The condition that controls the relationship expansion forwards from end 1 to end 2.
Expand 2-1 Condition - The condition that controls the relationship expansion backwards from end 2 to end 1.
Edge Definitions tab
The details of each edge definition is displayed in both the Target Access Group and Source Access Group panes in the following columns:
Edge Definitions Name - The name of the edge definition that defines the path from one starting object to one or more related objects.
Graph Definitions tab
The details of each graph definition is displayed in both the Target Access Group and Source Access Group panes in the following columns:
Graph Definitions Name - The name of the graph definition that consists of a set of interface definitions (nodes) connected by a set of edge definitions, which is used to define default expansions and alternate expansions for class
definitions. They are also used to define data sheets.
View Definitions tab
The details of each view definition is displayed in both the Target Access Group and Source Access Group panes in the following columns:
View Definitions Name - The name of the view definition that is based on a graph definition and uses the same relationship to its starting interface definition. View definitions are used to support the viewing of graphs as tables or in data
sheets.
Edit the target access group
You can edit the new or existing target access group to expand its functionality by adding objects from a source access group, such as methods or relationship definitions.
To relate objects to the Target Access Group:
1. Select the required objects from the Source Access Group list.
2. Click Relate Selected Item(s) 
 and the objects are added to the Target Access Group.
 If an object from the Source Access Group already exists in the Target Access Group, you will be asked if you want to override the existing object. For example, you want to add a method that has a condition in the source
access group and the method exists in the target access group, but does not have a condition. You can click Yes to accept the method, click No to continue the transfer, but not include the method, or click Cancel to completely stop the
transfer.
Search for all access group object types
You can search for all the objects available in the system, such as methods, which includes all the objects not currently displayed in the Source Access Group. The new objects display in a list in the Search Results column and can be
related to the target access group as required.
You cannot add duplicate objects to the target access group if any methods or relationship definition already have duplicates in the system. You can only select one unique object.
To display all the object types available in the system:
Type the name of the object in the Search All [Object Name] box, and click Search 
 or use wildcards to narrow the search.
The Source Access Group pane displays all objects available in the system in the Search Results[Object name] tab.
The Access Group column displays the name of any access groups in which the system object is currently allocated.
Filtering, Sorting and Saving Results
You can filter, sort, and save the edited results in both an access control query and an access group query.
Filter the query results
You can filter the displayed results in each of the access control or access group columns to narrow the results and get a manageable set of data.
Click Filter  on a column header, and select the criteria required. The list is update to match the criteria selected.
 If you can choose a conditioning value, such as Is equal to or Contains, when you click Filter the list is dynamically updated to only show rows that match the selected value.
Sort the query results
When you have a set of access control or access group results displayed in the grid view, you can sort each column list into either an ascending (A-Z) order or a descending (Z-A) order by clicking the column header.
Save the results
Click 
 Save when you have finished editing an access control query or access groups query. The changes are then applied to the SmartPlant Foundation database.
Additional Query Filters
The Additional Filters page displays an additional set of filters depending on the query type selected in the access control query wizard. You can select any of the additional filters to refine the query criteria. Each required filter can be
selected to display one of the following states:
 Selected - Returns items related to the filter.
 Unselected - Returns items that are not related to the filter.
 Blank - Returns all items (default).
 You can click Finish on a page in the wizard when the Finish button is highlighted. The query then returns all objects in the results grid.
General Filters
Three general filters are displayed for each query type:
Include role information - Displays the Role, Domain, and Owning Group columns in the results grid view.
Has access group relationship - Displays items related to an access group.
Container ID Filter - Type the container ID to filter by items containing the ID or use a wildcard to return all items.
Method filters
If you choose Methods as your starting object, the following Method Filter options display on the Additional Filters page:
Available in Desktop Client - Displays items available in the Desktop Client.
Has quick find item relationship - Displays items related to a quick find item.
Has menu item relationship - Displays items related to a menu item.
Has menu toolbar item relationship - Displays items related to a toolbar item.
Has drilldown item relationship - Displays items related to a drilldown item.
Has shortcut configured - Displays items that have a GUI shortcut item configured. Queries for Edge and Relationship Definitions also display this filter option.
Access Control Query Examples
The following examples show the wizard pages for a given query type. The pages displayed are dependent on the starting object and main criteria type selected for the data query:
Query for methods using By User Profile
1. Click the Methods pane at startup or select the Access Control for Methods in the drop-down list above the main results window.
2. On the Query Type page, click By User Profile.
3. On the Main Criteria type page, click the Users criteria type from the drop-down list.
4. Type the full name of the criteria to use for the query, Piping Engineer, and click Search 
.
5. Select Piping Engineer from the drop-down list, and click Next.
 Include Role Assignment only appears if you select Users as the main criteria type. If a method is available to the user in more than one configuration, the method may appear in multiple rows. Do not select this option if
you want to get a unique set.
6. On the Configuration Items page, click Select All from the list, and click Next.
7. On the Roles page, select Piping Engineer from the list, and click Next.
8. On the Access Groups page, click Select All or select the access groups individually, and click Next.
9. On the Client APIs page, click Select All or select the client APIs individually, and click Next.
10. On the Additional Filters page, select additional filter options to use for the query, and click Next.
11. On the Summary page, check if all the search query details are correct, and click Finish.
Query for relationship definitions using By Schema
1. Click the Relationship Definitions pane at startup or select the Access Control for Relationship Definitions in the drop-down list above the main results window.
2. On the Query Type page, click By Schema.
3. On the Main Criteria type page, select the Class Definitions criteria type from the drop-down list.
4. In the Main Criteria Name box, type the name of the class definition DEVTag, and click Search 
.
5. Click DEVTag from the drop-down list, and click Next.
6. On the Interface Definitions page, click Select All from the list, and click Next.
7. On the Additional Filters page, select additional filter options to use for the query, and click Next.
8. On the Summary page, check if the search query details are correct, and click Finish.
Query for methods using All Methods
1. Click the Methods pane at startup or select the Access Control for Methods in the drop-down list above the main results window.
2. On the Query Type page, click All Methods. This option is only available when you select Methods as the main starting object.
3. On the Client APIs page, select the client APIs to use as the filter criteria type, and click Next.
4. On the Additional Filters page, select additional filter options to use for the query, and click Next.
5. On the Summary page, check if the search query details are correct, and click Finish.
Query for Access Group
1. Click the Access Group pane at startup, or select the Manage Access Groups from the list in the main window, to open the Access Group Query Wizard.
2. In the Access Groups page, select Create a new Access Group in the Target Access Group pane.
3. Type details for the new access group in the Name and Description boxes, such as ProjectViewer.
4. In the Configuration Statuses list, select the required statuses, such as Approved, Completed, Finished, and Published.
5. In the Source Access Group box, type ProgressAdmin for the name of the source access group and click Search 
.
6. Select ProgressAdmin from the list, and click Next.
7. On the Summary page, check if the access group query details are correct, and click Back to make any changes.
8. Click Finish.
Access Control Diagram Examples
The following access control diagram examples show the various nodes that can be displayed in a diagram. Extra nodes are displayed for any GUI properties configured for a method, such as quick find, menu, and drilldown tree items.
DEVTagLightQFind method
The following example shows a diagram created for the DEVTagLightQFind method. The diagram shows the Quick find item and Menu item nodes.
SPFPlantDrillDown method
The following example shows a diagram created for the SPFPlantDrillDown method. The diagram shows the Drill down tree item node and the expanded SPFAccessGroupMethod details:
DevTagTag relationship definition
The following example shows a diagram created for the DEVTagTag relationship definition. It shows the expanded SPFRelDefAccessGroup interface nodes showing the expanded relationship details:
SmartPlant Attribute Manager
SmartPlant Attribute Manager can only be used to manage attributes in a system configured to contain a classification tree, related column sets, and a library of attributes created as column items. These items can then be managed
using Attribute Manager. For more information on the configuration of your system, see your system administrator.
SmartPlant Attribute Manager does not support modifications of document class definitions or document classifications.
SmartPlant Attribute Manager is an administration application that allows you to easily visualize and manage attributes that can be allocated to existing classifications configured in your system. Attribute Manager displays these attributes in
a matrix, which can be used to pick and organize the correct attributes for the right classification types. These attributes can then be displayed in dynamic sections in the appropriate form.
Each attribute represents a property definition or relationship definition exposed from an applicable interface definition modeled in SmartPlant Foundation. An attribute is persisted in the database as a column item, and may be associated
with any applicable column set. When an attribute is allocated to a column set, the display item is displayed in any forms that it has been assigned. Using SmartPlant Attribute Manager allows you to:
Visualize specific classifications, columns sets, and their attributes in an easily navigated grid view.
Edit the attributes for a column set to meet the schema requirements.
Manage the content of column sets and change how they dynamically generate display items on related forms.
Add existing attributes to specific classifications and their column sets.
Create new attributes and the supporting valid schema metadata for a column set.
Manage the sequence order of attribute column items as they appear in a column set.
Propagate an attribute sequence and apply the sequence to each column set in the grid view.
Create new sections and apply them to attributes to group them in sections in dynamic forms.
 For more information on sections and dynamic forms see Configure forms.
Get started in SmartPlant Attribute Manager
You can query for attribute data with SmartPlant Attribute Manager by selecting an object type as the main criteria and building a query using the Attribute Query By Classification wizard. The query dynamically locates the attributes in
the system that are applied to a classification or class definition and displays the attributes as column items in a results grid view.
To get started in SmartPlant Attribute Manager, log on to the SmartPlant Dashboard, and click the Attribution Manager tile on the main window.
The application starts and opens directly into the Attribute Query By Classification wizard.
Build an attribute query
You can build the query information you want using the Attribute Query By Classification wizard to display the attributes and column sets you want to view or modify. The attribute query defines the starting objects, which displays all the
applicable attributes and column sets in which they are used. You build the query based on the various main criteria you first select for the query, such as a:
Class Definition
Parent Classification
Child Classifications
The Attribute Query By Classification wizard displays the following main pages when you create an attribute query:
Main criteria page
The Main Criteria page is where you select a classification or class definition as the starting object type for your attribute query. Click the required option and select the classification or class definition object from a drop-down list, and click
Next.
 Click Check for updates if you have created any new attributes or classifications in the database.
Classification page
The Classification page is where you can select the classifications in a tree view to define the query to a return a targeted set of object attributes. You can select either a top-level parent classification or class definition, which selects all
the child classifications in the tree view or you can select just a parent or child classification object only. Click the required classification options from the tree view, and click Next.
 When a class definition is selected as the Main Criteria, only the classification options controlled by the selected class definition can be selected.
Summary page
The Summary page is where you confirm the details of the attribute query are correct or you can go back and edit the query and refine the details further if required. Click Finish to run the query.
 For examples of queries for attributes, see Attribution Manager Wizard Examples.
Explore the query results grid view
The objects returned from a query are displayed in rows in the results grid view. This view displays the details of each object in the following columns:
Classification Name
Classification Description (Hidden by default)
Column Set Name (Hidden by default)
Attributes (each shown in a separate column item)
How can I display the hidden columns?
The Classification Description and Column Set Name columns are hidden by default. Click Show description or Show column set name in the Display section of the Options pane. The columns expand to show the classification
descriptions or column sets in the results grid.
How can I refine the query results?
After running the query, click 
 Refine Query. Edit the details in the Attribute Query By Classification Wizard pages and rerun the query.
The following example shows the results grid for a class definition query, where some attributes have been modified, as shown by their legend color. However, the changes have not been saved to the database.
Modify attributes
You can modify the status of an attribute by clicking the cell to enable or disable the attribute for a specific classification and column set. You can also use various controls in the Options pane, such as Sort, Actions, and Display, to refine
the attributes displayed in the results grid view.
What does the status of selected cells signify?
The following table shows the status that each attribute cell can display in the standard attribute grid:
Attribute
Description
Status
Inactive attribute
Saved to database
Active attribute
Saved to database
Highlighted inactive attribute
Selected to be modified
Highlighted active attribute
Selected to be modified
Modified to an inactive attribute
Modified but not saved to the database
Modified to an active attribute
Modified but not saved to the database
 Each attribute that is modified is highlighted with the color that is set using the Legend color picker. Different colors can be assigned for the Manage attribute behavior mode. For more information on changing the legend colors,
see Modifying legend colors.
Selecting multiple cells
You can select multiple cells by:
Click attributes one at a time using the Ctrl key.
Select a cell and drag the cursor over the required cells in a row.
Select an entire row of attributes by clicking the cell at the start of each row.
Apply actions
You can modify selected attributes using options available in the Actions section.
Action
Description
Check Selected Items
Any selected attributes are checked and enabled.
Uncheck Selected Items
Any selected attributes are unchecked and disabled.
Propagate Sequence
The sequence numbers for the column items are propagated to any column set displayed in the grid view and the changes are applied to the
database. Any list views displayed in the Desktop Client are also displayed in the same sequence.
 To see the sequence numbers on each attribute column item, click Sequence in the Sort section.
Sort the attribute grid
You can sort the attributes displayed as column items using the options in the Sort section.
Sort option
Description
Alphabetic
The attributes are sorted in alphabetical order [A-Z].
Tab Name
The attributes that have been applied to the same dynamic section are grouped together by the same tab name.
Data Type
The attributes are sorted into columns grouped by data type, such as property, relationship definition, or edge definition.
Sequence
The attribute column items are displayed in the numbered sequence that they are configured to display on dynamic
forms and list views.
 When you create a dynamic section in a form, you have the option to set the Tab Name for the attribute. When the attributes are displayed, you can group the attributes according to their dynamic section tab name. For more
information see, Modify the attribute sequence.
Modify attribute relationship behavior
You can modify the attribute relationship behavior for any selected attribute column item. The cell color changes to indicate the relationship behavior set for the attribute column item.
To change the relationship behavior for an attribute column item:
1. Click Manage Attribute behavior in the Mode section.
2. Highlight each cell containing a checked attribute column item and apply one of the relationship behavior options from the Actions section.
Action
Description
Set As Mandatory
The relationship behavior is set as mandatory for the attribute column item.
Set As Read Only
The relationship behavior is set as read only on the attribute column item.
Set As Hidden (List View)
The relationship behavior for the attribute is set as hidden from appearing on any list view.
Set As Hidden (Dynamic Form)
The relationship behavior for the attribute is set as hidden from appearing on any dynamic forms.
Set As Hidden (Both)
The attribute is set as hidden from all dynamic forms or list views for the attribute column item.
Multiple behaviors
The attribute has multiple behaviors, such as Mandatory and Set As Hidden (List View).
 Any attribute set as hidden from any dynamic form or list view still remains in any column set where it is configured.
3. The colors change to match the relationship behavior selected.
 You cannot apply two relationship behaviors to a single attribute cell. However, if you do change the behavior for an attribute that has multiple relationship behaviors, any other relationship behavior is disabled by default. This is due
to configuration considerations that govern the behavior of relationships.
For more information on relationship behavior, see Relationship definition behavior rules.
For more information on changing the relationship behavior colors in the Legend, see Modifying legend colors.
Add existing attributes
You can add attributes that already exist in the system to selected classifications. The list of available attributes that are available is restricted by the original query criteria defined by the selected classification or class definition. To add an
existing attribute to the displayed classifications:
1. In the main toolbar, click Add Existing Attributes 
.
Any attributes that exist in the system that apply to the selected schema objects are shown in the Add Existing Attribute dialog box. The following columns display the attributes details:
Name
Description
Tab Name - The tab name assigned to the attribute. This can be used to group attributes if the option is set on the form
Property Definition - The property definition assigned to the attribute
Path Definition - The path to the attribute's location
2. Select one or more existing attributes from the list in the Add Existing Attributes dialog box, and click Finish.
The attribute grid view is updated with the new attribute column items, where you can select, modify, and refine their relationship behavior as required.
An error message displays if you try to modify an invalid attribute's relationship behavior. You cannot continue until all validation errors have been resolved. For more information on the validation of invalid attributes, see Save the
changes.
Edge definition column items are not supported.
Create new attributes
You can create new attributes that do not currently exist in the system and apply them to any of classifications displayed after running an attribute query. The new attributes appear as new column items for each of the displayed
classifications. To create a new attribute:
In the main toolbar, click Create New Attributes 
, which opens the Create New Attribute dialog box.
You can now build a new attribute as either a property definition or a relationship definition using various defined criteria and supporting schema objects.
Property definition attribute
When you create a property attribute the following pages are displayed:
Attribute Details page
In the Attribute Details page, create the attribute and select Property Definition as the Attribute Type. In the Attribute Details section, enter the attribute's name, description, and tooltip. You can also search and select a Dynamic
Form Section object from a drop-down list, and click Next.
Schema Details page
In the Schema Details page, enter the name and description of the attribute in the Property Definition section. Select various schema objects details in the Property Definition Information section, and click Next.
The most common property definition scopes are available in the Scoped By drop-down list. However, you can search for any other scopes in the system, such as units of measurement lists or enumerated lists.
The Name box is populated with the name entered in the Attribute Details page as it is mandatory.
 Before creating any schema objects, you must ensure that the schema object does not already exist in the system to avoid data duplication.
Summary page
In the Summary page, confirm that the details of the new attribute are correct or refine the details as required. Click Finish to create the new attribute.
Relationship definition attribute
When you create a relationship definition attribute the following pages are displayed:
Attribute Details page
In the Attribute Details page, select Relationship Definition as the Attribute Type. In the Attribute Details section, enter the name, description, and tooltip for the new attribute. You can also search and select the Dynamic Form
Section object from a drop-down list, and click Next.
Schema Details page
In the Schema Details page, enter the name and description for the new attribute in the Relationship Definition section. In the Relationship Definition Information section, select the interface definitions for the objects at End 1 and End
2. Select the required options, and click Next.
 Before creating any schema objects, you must ensure that the schema objects do not already exist in the system to avoid data duplication.
The Name box is populated with the name entered in the Attribute Details page as it is mandatory.
You can choose the direction of the relationship definition by selecting Direction is 12.
Advanced Settings
The Advanced Settings section allows you to specify the relationship definition role display names for the selected interface definitions and the cardinality details for the objects. This page is optional, as you can bypass the page and
continue directly to Finish.
 If you skip this page, default values are set on the relationship definition to ensure that the cardinality is Many to Many and the role names leverage the display names of the selected End 1 and End 2 Interface Definitions.
Summary page
In the Summary page, confirm that the details of the new attribute are correct or refine the details as required. Click Finish to create the new attribute.
 For examples of creating each type of new attribute, see Attribution Manager Wizard Examples.
Create new classifications
You can create a new classification that does not currently exist in the system and apply any of the attributes displayed in the grid view. The new classification is related to the existing classifications and column sets in the grid view and
appears as new row item at the bottom of the list.
In the main toolbar, click Create a new classification 
, and the Create Classification wizard opens and displays the following pages:
Parent Classification page
The Parent Classification page is where you start by selecting the parent classification from the tree view, and click Next.
Classification Details page
The Classification Details page is where you enter the name and description for the new classification, and click Next.
Summary page
The Summary page is where you confirm that the details of the new classification are correct or refine the details as required. Click Finish to create the new classification.
When you select a parent classification from the tree, the corresponding column set is automatically applied.
A new column set is created, when you create a new classification from a parent classification from the tree. You then add or create attributes to the new column set in the results grid view.
If the parent classification has a corresponding enumeration, you must create a new enumeration for a new classification. This will be an EnumListType, unless you select Create as EnumEnum.
For an example of creating a new classification, see Attribution Manager Wizard Examples.
Modify the attribute sequence
You can change the sequence that any attribute is displayed in the grid view and in sections on dynamic forms by modifying the sequence. You can also show attributes in context with other similar attributes by grouping them together.
 In order for any dynamic form sections to reflect attribute sequence changes, the Use Form Sequence Override option must be selected in the system options. The display items in dynamic sections are re-sequenced
according to any changes. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
To set the sequence for attribute column items:
1. In the main toolbar, click Set sequence or section for attributes in the current tree 
.
2. In the Set attribute sequence or tab dialog box, use the 
 or 
 arrows to move the attributes up or down in the sequence or click 
 or 
 to move the attribute to the top or to the bottom of the order.
For example, in the following image the attribute DEVTagCategory has been moved up to sequence number 12 and DEVYMD has been moved down to sequence number 16.
3. Click Auto Set Sequence and the attributes are set in the new sequence.
4. Click OK.
Group attributes by section name
Every attribute applied to a dynamic section appears with a tab name entry in the Section Name column. You can move and group attributes together that are used on the same section.
Add a section name
If an attribute is not assigned to a dynamic section, you can choose a tab name that already exists by clicking the corresponding cell in Section Name column, and selecting a tab name from the list.
Allocating an attribute to a dynamic section controls where the attribute appears on a dynamic form in the Desktop Client.
Create a new dynamic section
 SmartPlant Attribution Manager does not support the modification of dynamic sections for documents.
If an attribute is not assigned to a dynamic section, you can create a new dynamic section so it appears in the list of entries in the Section Name column:
1. Click Create Section.
2. Type the details in the Section Name, Section Display As, and Tab Name boxes.
 The Tab Name can be used in the attribute grid to group attributes together that are used in the same dynamic section. For more information, see Explore the query results grid view.
3. Click OK.
The new section appears in the Section Name column drop-down list.
 The attributes are sorted in the grid view to the sequence order set using the Sequence option in the Sort section. For more information, see Sort the attribute grid.
For more information on dynamic sections, see Dynamic sections.
Save the changes
When you finish editing the attributes for a selected classification, click Save 
 in the main toolbar.
What validation is done when I select save?
When you add an attribute to the attribute list, changes are validated when you click Save. Any validation errors that occur must be resolved before you can save the changes. You also cannot change mode to alter relationship behavior
until any errors have been resolved. This helps prevent invalid attributes from appearing on forms and lists.
Display dynamic section contents in the Desktop Client
In the Desktop Client, you can control whether a form displays the content of a dynamic section configured in SmartPlant Attribute Manager by setting the form's Allow Attribute Manager content property to True.
By default, the Allow Attribute Manager content property is set to False, so that any existing forms in the Desktop Client are not impacted by any changes made to a dynamic section using SmartPlant Attribute Manager. For more
information, see Dynamic sections.
Modifying legend colors
You can modify the legend colors using the color picker for each option in the Legend section. The colors indicate various cell states, such as when a cell is modified or invalid. To change the colors in the Legend:
1. In the Legends section, click 
 Down-Arrow against the relationship behavior status color you want to apply to selected cells.
2. Select one of the default colors or use the color picker sliders to match the required RGB color. Any attribute whose status is subsequently modified is shown highlighted with the new color.
For example, the following legend is the default for Manage relationship behavior mode. When you apply the changes, the attributes are displayed with the matching relationship behavior color.
Attribution Manager Wizard Examples
The following examples show the various wizard pages for running attribute class definition and classification queries, as well as creating attribute, relationship definitions, and classifications.
Query for attributes by class definition
1. Click Select class definition and choose the class definition DEVTag from the list, and click Next.
 Only class definitions that realize the ISPFClassifiedItem interface definition appear in the list.
2. Select the top classification, Equipment type level-0 (top level) from the tree view, and click Next.
3. View the Summary, and click Finish.
Query for attributes by classification
1. Click Select top classification and choose the DEV Equipment classification from the list, and click Next.
2. Select the top classification, Equipment type level-0 (top level) from the tree view.
3. Uncheck the parent classifications DEV Electrical Equipment and DEV Process Equipment, but check all the child classifications, and click Next.
4. View the Summary, and click Finish.
Create property definition attribute
1. In the Attribute Details page, select Property Definition in the Attribute Type section.
2. In the Attribute Details section, enter MaxPower as the attribute name and Maximum Power in the description and tooltip boxes.
3. In the Dynamic Form Section box, type the name of the dynamic section or use a search to find the dynamic section. Select Electrical from the list, and click Next.
4. In the Schema Details page, type the name and description previously entered for the new attribute in the Property Definition section.
 Before creating any schema objects, you must ensure that the schema object does not already exist in the system to avoid any data duplication.
5. In the Property Definition Information section, select IDevTag from the list in the Exposing Interface Definition box.
 The list only presents applicable interface definitions based on the class definitions in the results grid.
6. In the Scoped By box, enter the type of scope or use a search or select the scope from the list. For example, PowerUOM.
 The most common property definition scopes are available in the Scoped By list. However, you can search for any other scopes in the system, such as units of measurement lists or enumerated lists.
7. Click Next.
8. View the Summary, and click Finish.
Create relationship definition attribute
1. In the Attribute Details page, select Relationship Definition as the type of attribute in the Attribute Type section.
2. In the Attribute Details section, enter in the attribute name, description and tooltip boxes. For example, TagSupplier and Supplier Tag.
3. In the Dynamic Form Section box, type the name of the dynamic section or use a search to find the dynamic section. Select Purchasing from the list, and click Next.
